content:
First, rephrase the forbidden‐adjacency condition in graph‐theoretic terms.  Think of the 1000×1000 board as the vertex‐set of the grid graph G, whose edges join orthogonally adjacent cells.  A placement of non‐adjacent dominoes is then exactly an induced matching in G:  

–  “Matching’’ means no two dominoes share a cell (i.e. no two chosen edges share a vertex).  
–  “Induced’’ means no edge of G outside your matching has both ends in the union of matched vertices, i.e. no two dominoes even touch along an edge.  

We want the maximum size of an induced matching in G.

Key observation:  Partition the board into 2×2 blocks (there are (1000/2)·(1000/2)=500·500=250 000 of these).  In any single 2×2 block, one checks by exhaustion that you can place at most one domino without creating a forbidden adjacency:

Label a generic 2×2 block’s cells as  
 a  b  
 c  d  
and try to put in two dominoes—no choice of two disjoint edges avoids having one cell of one domino adjacent to one cell of the other.  Hence each 2×2 block can host at most one domino.

Since the 2×2 blocks tile the entire board and each can carry at most one domino, the total number of dominoes is at most 250 000.  On the other hand, the obvious “one domino per block’’ pattern (say, always cover a–b in each block) actually attains 250 000.  Therefore the maximum is

\boxed{250000}.
